identified_error: The paper’s key methodological error was treating age incorrectly in the time-to-event model: the pooled logistic regression adjusted only for age at cohort entry (baseline age), while follow-up extended for years and dementia risk is overwhelmingly driven by attained age (time-varying age). In an open cohort with staggered entry and calendar-year fixed effects, using baseline age instead of updating age over person-time mis-specifies the risk set and effectively compares people at very different attained ages under different wildfire exposures.
why_fatal: Dementia incidence rises steeply with age, and wildfire PM2.5 also varies over calendar time. Because exposure was a time-varying 3-year rolling mean updated quarterly, but age was not updated similarly, the model failed to properly control for the dominant confounder/time scale. This can create a spurious association if higher wildfire years coincide with later follow-up when participants are older and inherently at much higher dementia risk. In other words, the estimated wildfire effect can largely reflect uncontrolled attained-age effects and secular time patterns rather than a causal exposure effect. Since the headline conclusion depends on a relatively modest association, this age-time-scale error undermines the validity of the main findings and subgroup claims.
correct_approach: The analysis should have used attained age as the primary time scale, or at minimum included time-varying age (updated each quarter/person-period) in the pooled logistic model. Equivalent correct approaches would include a survival model with age as the underlying time axis, left truncation/delayed entry at cohort entry, and proper handling of time-varying exposures. Calendar time could then be modeled separately if needed. The core requirement is that each person-period’s hazard be compared among individuals at the same attained age, not just the same baseline age.
additional_errors: Open-cohort pooled logistic modeling should clearly align person-period exposure, censoring, and risk intervals; the manuscript description is sparse on that structure.
Potential residual confounding by socioeconomic status beyond tract poverty is substantial.
Outcome ascertainment by diagnostic codes may introduce misclassification, though this alone would not usually cause retraction.
Comparing per-1 μg/m3 effects for wildfire and nonwildfire PM2.5 is rhetorically misleading because their exposure distributions differ greatly.
Possible overinterpretation of subgroup heterogeneity, especially for small racial/ethnic subgroups with unstable estimates.
